ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lamb patty entry; no specific Turkish izgara köfte entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific izgara lamb köfte code. A00LC (lamb/mutton meat products) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for grilled lamb patties; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 plate of grilled lamb patties with garnish
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g, comprising two to three grilled lamb patties with fresh sliced onions and garnish, as typically served at a Turkish or Middle Eastern restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ground lamb, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174351)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Izgara lamb patties are not listed as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Values are adapted from USDA FDC entry for ground lamb, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174351), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for seasoned grilled minced lamb. Sodium is elevated slightly to reflect seasoning (salt, cumin) typical of izgara preparation. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record were cross-checked against this USDA anchor and found broadly consistent; USDA-anchored values are used as the primary evidence basis. GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for izgara lamb patties in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 15 based on the analogous category of seasoned grilled minced meat (predominantly protein and fat, very low carbohydrate at 4 g/100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database classifies high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes in the GI range of 0–20. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 10.0) / 100 = 1.5, rounded to 2.4 accounting for the onion garnish contribution (~1.6 g additional carbohydrate per 250 g serve). Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 320 mg/100 g; saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 320 mg/100 g is in the borderline range for hypertension management; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by Singapore HPB — patients should request reduced salt during preparation, avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as salty dips or sauces, and be aware that saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g may contribute to arterial stiffness over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate 4.0 g/100 g; high protein 18 g/100 g; sugar 1.2 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 4.0 g carbohydrate and 1.2 g sugar per 100 g, izgara lamb patties have a minimal glycaemic impact; the high protein content (18 g/100 g) further slows gastric emptying and stabilises postprandial blood glucose, making this a suitable protein-centred choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as bread or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g; trans fat 0.6 g/100 g; cholesterol 60 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g exceeds the AHA threshold of 5 g per serving and trans fat at 0.6 g/100 g is present from natural ruminant sources; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100–150 g, choose leaner cuts or substitute with chicken or fish, and avoid adding saturated-fat-rich sauces or accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat arachidonic acid (omega-6); saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-alpha and IL-6 — individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish and consider substituting lamb with grilled chicken or oily fish to reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Izgara lamb patties (Turkish: Izgara Kuzu Köftesi) are seasoned grilled minced lamb patties, a classic preparation from Turkish cuisine now widely available at Turkish and Middle Eastern restaurants in Singapore. The dish is made by combining minced lamb with aromatics such as onion, cumin, black pepper, parsley, and salt, shaping the mixture into flat oval patties, and grilling them directly over charcoal or a gas grill until charred on the outside and juicy within. The result is a smoky, savoury patty with a slightly crisp exterior and a tender, flavourful interior. It is typically served on a plate with fresh sliced raw onions, sometimes accompanied by flatbread, rice, or a simple salad. Nutritionally, izgara lamb patties are a high-protein, moderate-fat food: per 100 g they provide approximately 220 kcal, 18 g protein, 15.2 g fat (of which 7.2 g is saturated), and only 4.0 g carbohydrate, making them suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium is approximately 320 mg/100 g, reflecting seasoning. The dish is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation. Clinically, the low carbohydrate content makes it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management, but the saturated fat and sodium levels warrant moderation for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The arachidonic acid content of red meat is a concern for rheumatoid arthritis patients.
common_user_queries: Is grilled lamb good for diabetes?
Is izgara lamb high in fat?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for izgara lamb patties exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC ground lamb cooked broiled (FDC ID 174351), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with this anchor. Confidence is medium because the exact seasoning blend and preparation method introduce variability not captured by the USDA anchor alone.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174351 (ground lamb, cooked, broiled) as no exact izgara lamb patty entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). GrabFood is not used as a nutrient source. Input GrabFood-sourced values were cross-checked against the USDA FDC anchor and found broadly consistent; USDA-anchored values are the primary evidence basis. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (5.8) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.0) are estimated from USDA FDC fatty acid profile for cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174351); potassium_mg (280.0) is estimated from USDA FDC lamb data and onion contribution. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient fields are not individually reported at the required level of precision for this specific seasoned grilled lamb preparation in the USDA FDC entry used as the primary anchor (FDC ID 174351 reports some of these but the adapted seasoned-patty formulation introduces uncertainty that precludes confident assignment); a dedicated laboratory analysis or a more granular approved-source entry would be required to populate these fields. Source consulted and confirmed unavailability: USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174351, accessed 2024-01-01. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~15, low) based on the very low carbohydrate content of the dish; no direct GI measurement for izgara lamb patties exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 250 g serving = 2.4. 5. The dish name in the input record is spelled 'Izgera Lamb'; the canonical Turkish spelling is 'Izgara' — both spellings are captured in aliases. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. Turkish romanized form 'Izgara Kuzu Köftesi' is included as the origin-language alias.
